---
title: "What if UEFA qualifying used a Swiss system?"
subtitle: "Simulating a fairer road to the World Cup with a hierarchical Bayesian model"
description: >
  UEFA's group-stage qualifiers can hand two equally good teams very
  different fates. I rebuilt qualifying as a Swiss-system tournament,
  powered it with a Bayesian Poisson model, and ran it ten thousand
  times to ask a simple question: would it be fairer?
author: "Matteo [COGNOME]"
date: "2026-01-01"        # <-- aggiorna alla data di pubblicazione
categories: [football, bayesian, simulation, tournament-design]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  freeze: auto
bibliography: references.bib
---

::: callout-note
This is a companion write-up to a longer methodological paper. Here I keep the
maths light and let the charts do the arguing. The full model specification,
priors, and convergence diagnostics live in the paper. All the code and data are
on [GitHub](https://github.com/mat126/bayesian-uefa-swiss).
:::

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

DATA = "outputs"   # cartella con gli artefatti salvati dal notebook
mc      = pd.read_parquet(f"{DATA}/results_mc.parquet")
hist    = pd.read_parquet(f"{DATA}/simulation_history.parquet")
sq      = pd.read_parquet(f"{DATA}/strength_vs_qual.parquet")
params  = pd.read_parquet(f"{DATA}/team_params.parquet")
cal     = pd.read_parquet(f"{DATA}/calibration_data.parquet")

hist["points"] = 3 * hist["wins"] + hist["draws"]
N_SIMS = hist["sim"].nunique()
```

## The complaint nobody can prove

Every qualifying cycle some country misses out by a whisker and their fans spend
a year calling it unlucky. I think the instinct is right but the target is wrong.
People blame the draw, as if they were handed an impossibly hard group, when the
pot system makes a genuinely brutal group nearly impossible to assemble in the
first place. The real problem with group-stage qualifying is structural, and it
has less to do with who is in your group than with how little room the format
leaves you to recover.

A qualifying group is short. You play a small handful of opponents, home and away,
and that hands every single match an enormous weight. Drop points once, in a game
you were expected to win, and you can spend the rest of the campaign chasing a gap
that never closes, because there are barely any matches left in which to close it.
One bad night, and the whole qualification is compromised.

Seeding is supposed to keep things balanced by spreading strength evenly across
the groups, and mostly it does. When a group does turn out lopsided, it is usually
not bad luck at all but a seeding failure: a team that is genuinely strong gets
slotted into a lower pot, because the pots lean on rankings that do not always
reflect real quality. And the pots are locked in once, months before anyone kicks
a ball. Two teams of similar ability can still end up with schedules of very
different difficulty, and neither of them had any say in it.

A Swiss system takes aim at both problems at once. After the opening round you are
always paired against teams on your own points total, so you spend the tournament
playing opponents at your level rather than whoever the pots happened to assign. A
single slip stops being fatal: lose, and you drop into matches you can win, with
your final position reflecting a full run of balanced games instead of one costly
result. So here is the question I set out to answer. If UEFA ran qualifying as a
**Swiss-system tournament** instead of fixed groups, how much would the outcomes
change, and would the format really be *fairer*?

## The idea: borrow the format from chess

A Swiss system does something groups cannot. Instead of locking your opponents in
before the tournament starts, it pairs you round by round against teams on your own
points total, with each round's matchups decided only once the previous one is over.
Win early and you climb into harder matchups; stumble and you drop into easier ones.
Nobody is ever quarantined in a "group of death," because there are no groups and no
fixed draw. The field self-sorts as it goes.

Chess has used this for over a century to rank hundreds of players in a handful of
rounds, and the idea is no longer foreign to football's own governance either. In
2024 UEFA abolished the Champions League group stage and replaced it with a single
36-team league table, a change it marketed as a "Swiss system." But it is only half
a Swiss system. The opponents are still drawn up front from seeded pots, before a
ball is kicked, and the full schedule is fixed from day one. What UEFA borrowed was
the single-table structure, not the round-by-round pairing that makes a Swiss system
fair, the part where matchups adapt to results as they happen instead of being locked
in by a seeding that, as we have seen, does not always get the pecking order right.
UEFA left that part on the table, and it is exactly the part I want to put back.

The appeal, for a fairness question, is what that pairing does over a full
tournament: it makes each team's schedule track its own level. You end up facing
opponents at your strength almost by construction, so the difficulty of your road
depends on how you play rather than on where the draw happened to place you. That is
precisely the property the group format cannot guarantee, and precisely the one I
set out to measure.

## Building the tournament engine

The simulator handles the 54 eligible UEFA nations (Russia is suspended) over eight
rounds. The mechanics that matter:

- **Swiss pairings** with no rematches. With an even field of 54 teams every round
  pairs cleanly, so no byes are needed.
- **Home/away balancing**, so no team is stuck playing everyone away.
- **Geopolitical restrictions**, so pairings that cannot happen in reality
  (Spain–Gibraltar, Armenia–Azerbaijan, Kosovo–Serbia) are avoided.
- A standard tiebreak stack: points, wins, Sonneborn-Berger, Buchholz, goal
  difference, goals for, FIFA ranking.

The one thing the engine does not decide on its own is the result of each match.
That is a plug-in. The simulator asks a probability function for the odds of a home
win, draw, and away win, and simulates from there:

```{python}
#| eval: false
def prob_fn(home_team, away_team) -> tuple[float, float, float]:
    # returns (p_home_win, p_draw, p_away_win)
    ...

SwissTournament(teams=teams, prob_fn=prob_fn).run()
```

A placeholder version of that function just uses the FIFA ranking gap. It is fine
for testing the plumbing but a weak forecaster. The interesting work is in what
fills the slot, which I will come to. First, a problem that has nothing to do with
the model and everything to do with how you decide who qualifies.

## Qualifying, and the problem with a hard cut line

Say twelve nations qualify directly for the World Cup and the rest fight through a
play-off. The obvious way to run this is to rank all 54 teams at the end and draw a
line: the top twelve go straight through, and a band below them enters the play-off.
Clean on paper. In practice the line lands in a terrible place.

In an eight-round Swiss the points pile up in narrow bands, and near the cut they
barely separate at all. Across `{python} f"{N_SIMS:,}"` simulations the twelfth-placed
team averages `{python} round(hist[hist['pos']==12]['points'].mean(),1)` points and
the thirteenth `{python} round(hist[hist['pos']==13]['points'].mean(),1)`, a gap of a
quarter of a point. More to the point:

```{python}
#| label: tie-stat
#| echo: false
piv = hist.pivot_table(index="sim", columns="pos", values="points")
p_tie_12_13 = (piv[12] == piv[13]).mean()
p_tie_10 = (piv[10] == piv[12]).mean()
from IPython.display import Markdown
Markdown(
    f"In **{p_tie_12_13:.0%}** of simulations the team that qualifies directly and "
    f"the team that just misses finish level on points. The cut does not separate a "
    f"stronger team from a weaker one. It separates two teams with the same record, on "
    f"goal difference or a tiebreak, and sends one to the World Cup and the other to a "
    f"play-off."
)
```

That is the arbitrariness the whole exercise was meant to remove, sneaking back in at
the last step. And it reaches surprisingly far up the table. The tied band around the
cut is usually four or five teams wide, so a side sitting as high as tenth can share
the cut's points total: it happens in almost half of all simulations
(`{python} f"{p_tie_10:.0%}"`). Drawing a hard line at position twelve would send some
of those teams through and drag others down, on nothing more than goal difference.

::: {#fig-points-cut}
```{python}
#| label: fig-points-cut-code
#| fig-cap: "Points by final position across all simulations, for the positions around the direct-qualification cut. The dashed line sits after twelfth place. Notice how the boxes on either side of it overlap almost completely: a hard cut here separates teams that are, on points, indistinguishable."
import plotly.express as px

band = hist[hist["pos"].between(8, 20)]
fig = px.box(band, x="pos", y="points",
             labels={"pos": "Final position", "points": "Points"})
fig.add_vline(x=12.5, line_dash="dash", line_color="crimson")
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, margin=dict(t=10))
fig.show()
```
:::

The fix is not mine to invent, because formats that run Swiss into a play-off have
lived with this for years and solved it. In competitive Pokémon (the VGC circuit) and
in chess, the cut to the next stage is not a rank, it is a record. Everyone who
finishes with at most a set number of losses advances, and the bracket that follows is
**seeded by final standing**. The line is drawn between record tiers, never between
two tied competitors.

Ported to qualifying, that gives a cleaner design. Direct qualification is reserved
for the top tier, the teams clearly clear of the contested band. Everyone in that
band drops into the play-off, but they carry their standing with them as a seed. That
seeding is the whole point. A tenth-placed team pulled down by a points tie does not
enter the play-off as an afterthought; it enters as a top seed, which in football
terms means a home tie against the weakest side to reach the play-off. It is dragged
in, but it is dragged in holding the best hand.

This keeps faith with the premise the format started from. One result no longer flips
you from *in* to *out*. It moves you from a direct ticket to a favourable play-off,
and where you finished still counts, now as a seed rather than as a coin toss on goal
difference. Exactly how many teams qualify directly, how wide the play-off tier runs,
and how many play-off winners go through are all design levers I can turn. What matters
is the principle: the cut should respect tiers, not slice through teams that finished
level.

## The engine under the hood: a Bayesian Poisson model

::: callout-tip
## You can skip this section
If you only care about the tournament results, jump ahead. This is the part that
turns "team A plays team B" into a credible score distribution.
:::

Football scores are counts, so a Poisson model is the natural starting point. Each
team gets an attacking strength and a defensive strength, and the expected goals in a
match come from the attack of one side against the defence of the other, plus a home
advantage term:

$$
\log(\lambda_{ij}) = \mu + \text{att}_i + \text{def}_j + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{home}]
$$

This is the lineage of @maher1982 and @dixoncoles1997, made Bayesian and hierarchical
after @baioblangiardo2010. Two design choices matter. The strengths are **partially
pooled**, so thin-data teams like San Marino are pulled toward the population mean
rather than fit wildly on a handful of lopsided results, and the likelihood carries an
**exponential time decay**, so last autumn's form counts for more than a match from
four years ago, with friendlies downweighted against competitive fixtures.

The result is one attack and one defence parameter per nation.

::: {#fig-team-params}
```{python}
#| label: fig-team-params-code
#| fig-cap: "Posterior attack (α) and defence (β) parameters for each nation. Sign conventions follow the model above; read positions relative to the cloud rather than as absolute quality."
fig = px.scatter(params, x="Alpha", y="Beta", text="Squadra",
                 labels={"Alpha": "Attack (α)", "Beta": "Defence (β)"})
fig.update_traces(textposition="top center", marker=dict(size=7))
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=10))
fig.show()
```
:::

Before trusting any of it I check that the model predicts real matches well. The chart
below bins held-out predictions and plots the predicted probability against the
observed frequency of each outcome; a well-calibrated model sits on the diagonal.

::: {#fig-calibration}
```{python}
#| label: fig-calibration-code
#| fig-cap: "Calibration on held-out matches. Points on the diagonal mean the model's stated probabilities match reality."
import plotly.graph_objects as go

fig = go.Figure()
edges = np.linspace(0, 1, 11)
for col, out, name in [("pred_home", "H", "Home win"),
                       ("pred_draw", "D", "Draw"),
                       ("pred_away", "A", "Away win")]:
    cal["_bin"] = pd.cut(cal[col], bins=edges)
    grp = (cal.groupby("_bin", observed=True)
              .apply(lambda d: pd.Series({"pred": d[col].mean(),
                                          "obs": (d["observed"] == out).mean()}))
              .dropna())
    fig.add_scatter(x=grp["pred"], y=grp["obs"], mode="markers+lines", name=name)
fig.add_scatter(x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1], mode="lines",
                line=dict(dash="dash", color="grey"), showlegend=False)
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Predicted probability",
                  yaxis_title="Observed frequency", margin=dict(t=10))
fig.show()
```
:::

```{python}
#| label: rps
#| echo: false
import numpy as np

_order = ["H", "D", "A"]   # scala ordinale: casa, pareggio, trasferta

def _rps(pred_cols, observed):
    P = cal[pred_cols].to_numpy()
    O = (pd.get_dummies(observed)
           .reindex(columns=_order, fill_value=0)
           .to_numpy().astype(float))
    Pc, Oc = np.cumsum(P, axis=1), np.cumsum(O, axis=1)
    return ((Pc[:, :-1] - Oc[:, :-1]) ** 2).sum(axis=1).mean() / (len(_order) - 1)

rps_model = _rps(["pred_home", "pred_draw", "pred_away"], cal["observed"])
_base = (cal["observed"].value_counts(normalize=True)
             .reindex(_order).fillna(0).to_numpy())
for _c, _v in zip(["b_home", "b_draw", "b_away"], _base):
    cal[_c] = _v
rps_base = _rps(["b_home", "b_draw", "b_away"], cal["observed"])
```

::: callout-note
## Validation
On `{python} len(cal)` held-out matches the model scores a Ranked Probability Score of
**`{python} f"{rps_model:.3f}"`**, against **`{python} f"{rps_base:.3f}"`** for a baseline
that always predicts the historical frequency of a home win, draw, and away win. Lower is
better, so the model cuts the naive error by roughly 30%. Sampling converged cleanly, with
all R-hat at 1.00 and no divergent transitions.
:::

The point to keep straight: I validate the *model* on matches that exist, because a
Swiss qualifying campaign has never been played and never can be observed. Once the model
earns trust on real football, I use it as the engine to project a format that does not.

## Running it ten thousand times

A single simulated campaign is one story. To learn anything I need the distribution of
stories, so the model feeds the simulator and the whole tournament runs under Monte
Carlo, recording where each nation finishes every time.

```{python}
#| eval: false
results = []
for _ in range(N_SIMULATIONS):
    tournament = SwissTournament(teams=fresh_teams(), prob_fn=bayesian_prob_fn)
    results.append(tournament.run(verbose_rounds=False))
```

I ran `{python} f"{N_SIMS:,}"` simulations, enough that each nation's qualification
probability is stable to well within a percentage point.

## What the Swiss system produces

Here is each nation's chance of qualifying directly and of reaching the play-off.

::: {#fig-qual-probs}
```{python}
#| label: fig-qual-probs-code
#| fig-cap: "Probability of direct qualification and of reaching the play-off, by nation."
top = mc.sort_values("P(qualif.)")
fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_bar(y=top["Squadra"], x=top["P(diretta)"], name="Direct", orientation="h")
fig.add_bar(y=top["Squadra"], x=top["P(playoff)"], name="Play-off", orientation="h")
fig.update_layout(barmode="stack", xaxis_title="Probability", yaxis_title="",
                  legend_title="", height=900, margin=dict(t=10))
fig.show()
```
:::

No surprises at the extremes. The strongest sides qualify in almost every simulation
and the weakest almost never do. The story lives in the middle, among the teams whose
fate is genuinely in play, and that is where a change of format has room to matter.

## Is it actually fairer?

This is where "fairer" has to stop being a feeling and become a number. The cleanest
version of the question is whether the format rewards being good. If it is fair, a
team's chance of qualifying should rise steadily with its underlying strength, and
little else should get in the way.

::: {#fig-strength-qual}
```{python}
#| label: fig-strength-qual-code
#| fig-cap: "Estimated attacking strength against probability of qualifying, one point per nation. The tighter and steeper this relationship, the more the format sorts teams by merit rather than by luck."
fig = px.scatter(sq, x="alpha", y="p_qual", text="team",
                 labels={"alpha": "Estimated strength (posterior α)",
                         "p_qual": "P(qualify)"})
fig.update_traces(textposition="top center", marker=dict(size=8))
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=10))
fig.show()
```
:::


## Where this breaks down

I would rather tell you the limits than have you find them.

The market-value features are a current snapshot, not a match-by-match history, so a
team's estimated quality does not travel back in time correctly. Team strengths are
also static within a campaign: real form drifts over a qualifying cycle and the model
does not follow that drift. And the deepest caveat is unavoidable. I am simulating a
world in which 54 nations play a format they have never played. The model is trained on
the football that exists, and I am asking it to extrapolate to football that does not.
That is a reasonable thing to do, but it is an extrapolation, and the fairness
conclusions are conditional on it.

## Takeaways

Two things come out of this cleanly. The model earns its keep, scoring well below a
naive baseline on matches it never saw, and a Swiss qualifying campaign sorts the
nations largely in order of their strength: qualification probability rises smoothly
with estimated ability, which is the basic thing you want a fair format to do. The
strongest sides get through, the weakest do not, and not much in between hangs on an
accident of the draw.

The sharper lesson is smaller than the one I set out to find. A Swiss system does not
hand you fairness for free. Run naively it smuggles back the very arbitrariness it was
meant to remove, because points bunch so tightly that the qualifying line almost always
falls between teams that finished level. Borrowing the record-tier cut and seeded
play-off from chess and competitive Pokémon repairs that, and keeps faith with the
premise the whole thing rests on: one bad night should cost you a seed, not your place.

Whether all of this adds up to a format that is genuinely fairer than the group draw is
the question I have not fully settled here. Sorting by merit is necessary but it is not
the whole of fairness, and the honest head-to-head against the real groups, with a
fairness measure I actually trust, is the next piece of work. For now the takeaway is
narrower and, I think, more interesting than a verdict: a Swiss World Cup qualifier is
buildable, it behaves sensibly, and its hardest problem turns out to be not the football
but where you draw the line.

The code, the model, and every chart in this post are reproducible from the
[repository](https://github.com/mat126/bayesian-uefa-swiss). If you spot something wrong, I would like to know.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
